sources_audited.note: The cited Census NAICS page loaded, but its fetched content did not expose the requested 33272/332722 definition or the registered wording, so the claim could not be confirmed from the page content in this review.
The BLS NAICS 332 page reports the five stated 2025 occupation counts exactly; it is explicitly a broad subsector source rather than evidence specific to 332722 or the retained lens.
The cited ILO PDF returned HTTP 403 when reopened, so the registered occupational-exposure percentages could not be independently verified.
The Census graphic reports 51% age 55 or older and clearly limits the estimate to responding owners of employer businesses in the 2019 Annual Business Survey for data year 2018.
FRED reports BLS real sectoral output for NAICS 3327 at 102.578 in 2022 and 84.601 in 2025, with index 2017=100 and annual frequency.
BLS reports a 7% projected decline for metal and plastic machine workers from 2024 to 2034 and states that firms are expanding CNC tools and robots to improve quality and lower production costs.

caveats: S1's fetched page did not display the registered NAICS definition, leaving the definitional support for e and o unconfirmed; those primitives are already estimates and removing this background support would not change the base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

S3 was unreachable because the cited ILO PDF returned HTTP 403. The affected a primitive is already labeled PROXY, and downgrading its support would not by itself change the base tier or action category.
S2 supplies exact occupation counts for all NAICS 332 but no occupation-share denominator or fastener-specific task mix, so the numeric a range remains a judgmental bridge rather than a measurement.

The d5 bridge also quotes a Federal Reserve industrial-production decline that is not supported by S5 or another registered source. S5 does support the separately stated BLS real-output decline; removing the uncited corroborating statistic does not change the base tier or action.

S6 concerns a broad national occupation and conventional CNC/robot automation, not AI implementation in eligible fastener plants; its use for a and rho remains directional and is appropriately limited by PROXY/ESTIMATE treatment.
summary: Mechanics pass. The record is useful and internally coherent: the narrowed contract/qualified-program lens is disclosed, uncertain primitives are honestly labeled, ranges are broad enough to show sensitivity, and the STRUCTURAL_OUT base conclusion follows from weak labor opportunity despite strong retention and demand factors. Two cited sources could not be fully verified in fetched content and one bridge includes an uncited corroborating statistic, but none of those issues passes the required test for changing the base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.
